turns.speaker: host
turns.text: Welcome to the show. Today we're diving into algorithmic bias — how AI systems can perpetuate discrimination in hiring, criminal justice, and beyond. I'm thrilled to have you here to help us understand why this matters so deeply.

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: Thanks for having me. The core issue is that AI doesn't just reflect bias—it can amplify it. Take Amazon's hiring tool: trained on a decade of male-dominated resumes, it learned to penalize anything associated with women, including "women's college." The machine didn't just mirror history, it automated discrimination.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: That Amazon example is chilling. So is the problem mainly that these systems learn from our biased history, or are there other ways bias sneaks into the algorithm itself?

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: Biased history is just one entry point. Representation bias is another major culprit—facial recognition systems trained on datasets overrepresenting lighter-skinned faces had error

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: rates up to thirty-five percent for darker-skinned women compared to under one percent for lighter-skinned men. That's a staggering gap—what happens when these flawed systems get deployed in real-world settings like law enforcement?

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: In criminal justice, these errors become life-altering. Consider COMPAS, a recidivism algorithm that falsely flagged Black defendants as high-risk at nearly twice the rate of white defendants. When flawed AI informs bail or sentencing decisions, it doesn't just make mistakes—it systematically disadvantages already over-policed communities.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: So we have these deeply flawed systems making life-altering decisions. How do we even begin to define what a "fair" algorithm looks like—is there consensus on measuring fairness, or is that part of the problem too?

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: Consensus is exactly the problem—fairness metrics actually conflict. Demographic parity demands equal selection rates across groups, while equalized odds demands equal accuracy across groups. You often can't satisfy both simultaneously, forcing us to make value judgments about which kind of fairness matters most in a given context.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: That's such a profound tension—there's no mathematical escape from choosing our values. As we wrap up, what gives you hope that we can navigate these trade-offs and build AI systems that actually serve justice?

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: What gives me hope is that we're no longer flying blind. Researchers have identified distinct bias categories—histor
